chunk_summary: Guidelines for complementary feeding in preterm infants emphasize readiness by corrected age, with 6 months as the default in high infection-risk settings, focusing on nutrient density and developmental milestones.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of corrected age in determining complementary feeding readiness for preterm infants.
Identify developmental readiness criteria for initiating solids in former preterm infants.
Learn safe feeding practices, including texture competence and nutrient-dense food introduction.
Recognize the role of milk as the primary nutrition source during early complementary feeding.
key_facts: Complementary feeding should begin around 6 months corrected age for preterm infants, especially in high infection-risk settings.
Randomized evidence showed no growth advantage with earlier feeding at 4 months corrected age and reported higher hospital admissions.
Readiness criteria include good head and neck control, diminished tongue thrust, interest in food, and stable respiratory status.
Iron-dense foods should be introduced early and repeatedly due to higher iron needs in preterm infants.
Milk remains the primary nutrition source during early complementary feeding, with solids aimed at skill development and nutrient density.
reference_quote: Randomized Indian evidence comparing complementary feeding start at 4 versus 6 months corrected age did not show growth advantage with earlier feeding and reported higher hospital admissions in the earlier-start group. (JAMA Pediatrics)
document_key_topics: Corrected age and feeding readiness in preterm infants
Complementary feeding protocols and nutrient density
Developmental milestones for safe feeding initiation
